v  Blockchain is a foundational  technology used primar-
i
ily in decentralized currency systems such as Bitcoin [1].
X  In blockchain systems,  transactions are processed in units
r
known as blocks. The creation of a block involves numerous
a
hash calculations, a process referred to as mining. Nodes
that perform mining are called miners. Each miner follows a
fork choice rule to identify and extend the main chain. When
miners successfully create a block, they may be rewarded via
a coinbase transaction, by which they would receive what is
known as a block reward. However, these block rewards are
obtainable only when the blocks that have been generated
become part of the main chain.
Mining fairness refers to equality between the computa-
tional resources invested in mining and the resulting block
rewards. In other words, it is equality between the proportion
of hashrate and the proportion of block rewards (hereafter
referred to as  the block reward rate).  If all blocks were
incorporated into the main chain, mining fairness would be
achieved because the number of blocks generated by each

miner would not be affected by the state of the network.
However, in practice, not all blocks are included in the main
chain because of blockchain forks, and when blocks are
discarded as a result, mining fairness is compromised. Forks
can be classified into two types:  intentional  (malicious)
and unintentional. The latter occurs when multiple blocks
are generated almost simultaneously. This study addresses
mining fairness in the context of unintentional forks.
Mining  fairness  introduces  a  tradeoff  between  the
transaction processing  capacity  and decentralization  in
blockchain systems (Fig. 1); increasing the transaction pro-
cessing capacity of a system compromises decentraliza-

consecutive orange dotted lines represents a single round in
the blockchain.
simplifies the analysis of complex blockchain networks.
Afterward, we validate the accuracy of our proposed
model-based calculation in measuring mining fairness by
conducting simulation experiments. Owing to computational
constraints, validating our method in large-scale networks
would be challenging.  Instead, we perform the validation
in networks with small numbers of miners. Our  results
demonstrate that the model-based calculation quantitatively
determines mining fairness much more accurately than ex-
isting methods.
The model-based calculation enables efficient and com-
prehensive analysis of mining fairness.  In this study, we
analyze mining fairness using the model-based calculation.
First, we examine mining fairness  in a simple network
composed of two miners. Subsequently, we investigate the
impact of hashrate distribution on mining fairness. Finally,
we reassess existing blockchain network improvement meth-
ods from the perspective of mining fairness.
2. Rounds
Herein, to accurately capture and incorporate the impact
of forks, we introduce the concept of rounds in a blockchain.
A round is a time interval defined from a global perspec-
tive. Specifically, round r refers to the time from the first
generation of a block at height r to the first generation of a
block at height r+1. The height of round r is defined as r.
Because  forks  occur  probabilistically  within  a
blockchain network,  the  number  of  blocks  per  round
is not always equal  to one. After the first block at height
r has been generated, another miner may generate a new
block before the first block is propagated throughout  the
network. It should be noted that not all blocks generated in
round r have a height of r. For example, a miner unaware
of the block at height r − 1 will generate a block at height
r − 1 during round r (Fig. 2).
The round start  rate of each miner  is defined as  the
probability that  the miner initiates a round. Note that  the
round start rate does not equal the proportion of the hashrate,
owing to the occurrence of forks.
With the introduction of rounds, we can formally define
the fork rate. The fork rate is the probability that the number
of blocks per round is two or more. When the number of
blocks per round is two or more, the blockchain diverges, or

3.1. Model
We approximate a complex blockchain network using
a simplified model  to calculate mining fairness. First, we
define the set of miners as V , and let N be the number of
elements in V . The proportion of the hashrate of miner i (i ∈
V ) is denoted by αi. When a new block is generated within
the network, the probability that miner i has generated that
block is equal to the proportion of their hashrate, αi. The
number of blocks generated in each round is assumed to
be at most two, implying that there is at most one fork per
round. We assume that the block rewards are equal.
Let Fij be the probability that miner j generates a block
that causes a fork within round r started by miner i. After
a fork occurs, as additional blocks are generated, one of the
blocks will be incorporated into the main chain, while the
other will not be. Let Wij be the probability that the block
generated by miner i is incorporated into the main chain
under the conditions that (a) round r starts with the block
generated by miner i and (b) miner j generates a block that
causes a fork.
3.2. Definition of Mining Fairness
Before calculating mining fairness, we first  have  to
properly define it. In this study, mining fairness is divided
into local mining fairness and global mining fairness. We
define local mining fairness, LF , based on two measures as
follows:
LF1(i) = ri − αi,  (1)
LF1(i)
LF2(i) =  ,  (2)
αi
where ri refers to the block reward rate for each miner, LF1
denotes the profit of each miner, and LF2 denotes the profit
rate of each miner.
Next, we define global mining fairness, GF , using local
mining fairness, as follows:

and GF2 is the maximum difference in the profit rates. Other
mining fairness measures can also be defined using LF .
3.3. Calculation of Mining Fairness
This section demonstrates a computational method for
mining fairness based on the previously presented model.
First, we determine the round start rate. Next, we calculate

the local mining fairness LF1, which is the difference be-
tween each miner’s block reward rate and the proportion
of the hashrate. We also determine each miner’s profit rate
LF2. After the local mining fairness has been calculated,
the global mining fairness can be easily determined.

generates the block that starts round r. Then, the following
equation holds:

3.4. Algorithm
In this section, we describe the algorithm used in this
study to calculate mining fairness, as detailed in Section 3.3.
Algorithm 1 employs an iterative method to compute
the mining fairness of each miner. The round start  rate
calculation is performed calculated between lines 9 and 27.
Specifically, The fork rate is precomputed between lines 10
and 16. The variable loop manages the operations executed
in each iteration.

The calculations within the for loop from
lines 20 to 26 follow the same process as described in (5).
Mining fairness is computed between lines 28 and 34, with
the for loop calculations corresponding to equations (8) and
(9).

Algorithm 1 Calculation of local mining fairness
The following variables are provided by the model:
1: V  ▷ set of miners
2: N  ▷ number of miners
3: α[N]  ▷ proportion of hashrate
4: F [N][N]  ▷ fork rate
5: W[N][N]  ▷ winning rate
Our goal is to calculate the following values:
6: π[N][2]  ▷ round start rate
7: LF1[N]  ▷ LF1
8: LF2[N]  ▷ LF2

1) mod 2] + αi(1 − Fji)π[j][loop]

1) mod 2] + dp[j]αiπ[j][loop]
25:  end for

Calculating the local fairness for each miner:

29:  LF1[i] ← π[i][(loop + 1) mod 2] − α[i]
30:  for j ∈ V do
31:  LF1[i] ← LF1[i] + π(j)αiFji(1 − Wji) −
π(i)αjFij(1 −Wij)
32:  end for
33:  LF2[i] ← LF1[i]/α[i]
34:  end for

3.5. Parameters

block generation interval, and let Tij represent the time it
takes for a block generated by miner i to be received by
miner j. Then, Fij is determined as follows:

3.5.2. How to determine Wij . Prior to any explanations
regarding Wij , it is first essential to discuss the concept of
chain ties.
Each miner constructs chains  from their blocks and
selects one main chain among them. The rule for selecting
this chain is known as the fork choice rule. In Bitcoin, for
example,  the longest chain rule, which selects the longest
chain, is adopted.
However,  in some cases,  the fork choice rule alone
may not uniquely determine the main chain owing to the
occurrence of forks. This situation is known as a chain tie.
To resolve a chain tie, a tie-breaking rule is implemented.
In Bitcoin, for example, the first-seen rule, which selects the
chain received first, is adopted.
We categorize practical tie-breaking rules as follows:
First-seen rule
Selects  the earliest arriving chain among the
chains in a tie. Used in Bitcoin.
Random rule
Randomly selects a chain among the chains in a
tie [4]. Proposed as a countermeasure to selfish
mining. Used in Ethereum.
Last-generated rule
Selects the latest chain among the chains in a
tie [17],  [18]. Suppresses selfish mining more
effectively than the random rule.
Next, we explain how to determine Wij . The value of
Wij  is significantly influenced by the hashrate of miners
mining on the block generated by miner i during a fork.
More specifically, Wij  is largely affected by the following
two factors:
Tie-breaking rule
During a fork, chain ties often occur. The tie-
breaking rule determines  the block on which
miners,  other  than  the block generator, will
mine.
Proportion of hashrate of the block generator
The block generator mines on its own generated
block regardless of the tie-breaking rule.
Other factors, such as the block propagation time and the
number of miners participating in the network, also influence
Wij . Section 4 provides further details on specific methods
for calculating Wij ..

Herein, we validate  the capability of  our  proposed
model-based calculation to accurately determine mining fair-
ness. First, we examine the assumption that the number of
blocks per round is at most two from the perspective of the
scale of the fork. Next, we compare the results of simulation
experiments with those of the model-based calculation.
Whereas it  is excessively time-consuming to calculate
mining fairness via simulations of networks composed of
many (approximately 100 or more) miners, it is feasible to
calculate mining fairness accurately and relatively quickly
for networks with a small number of miners (2–10). In this
study, we perform the validation using networks consisting
of two and ten miners and show that our proposed model-
based calculation determines mining fairness much more
accurately than existing methods.
4.1. Examining the Scale of Forks
The model-based calculation disregards the impact of
large-scale forks. Specifically, it assumes that the number of
blocks per round is at most two. In this study, we investigate
the effects of large-scale forks.
Regarding the Scale of Forks First, we establish some
facts regarding fork rates. Let the hashrate of miner i, where
i ∈ V , be Mi. Let  the total network hashrate be Mall.
Additionally,  let the probability of successfully generating
a block with one hash calculation be p. Then, the average
number of hash calculations required to generate a block is
1/p. Therefore, the following equation holds:

4.2.2. Simulation Settings. Based on the blockchain net-
work simulator SimBlock [19], we developed another event-
driven simulator composed of two miners. Our simulator is
capable of simulating forks of any scale, similar to those in
real blockchain systems.
We examined variations in d/T ,  i.e.,  the ratio of  the
block propagation time to the average block generation inter-
val, with values of 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5. The block propagation
time was kept constant among all miners.
We also examined variations in αA, i.e., the proportion
of hashrate of miner A, with values of 0.1 and 0.3. A value
of 0.5 was not considered because,  in this case, mining
fairness is completely maintained owing to the symmetry
of the network.
Each simulation consisted of 10 billion rounds.
4.2.3. Validation Results. The errors between the simula-
tion and model-based calculation results are listed in Table
2. Here, error is defined as the relative error as follows:

each miner, and LFMBC is the vector of the model-based
}
calculated values of local mining fairness.
Based on the error values, it can be seen that the model-
based calculation can compute mining fairness with high
accuracy. It can also be observed that the accuracy deteri-
orates as d/T increases.

This is likely because, as seen in
Section 4.1,  the impact of having more than three blocks
per round becomes more significant as d/T increases.
4.3. Network Composed of 10 Miners
In this section, we validate the model-based calculation
of mining fairness on a network composed of 10 miners.
Compared to a network with two miners, a network with ten
miners introduces additional elements such as tie-breaking
rules,  hashrate distribution,  and block propagation time.
This allows us  to demonstrate that  the proposed model-
based calculation method is effective even in more complex
networks.

4.3.1. Wij in a Network Composed of Multiple Miners.
In a network with more than two miners,  it  is necessary
to consider tie-breaking rules. Here, we demonstrate how to
determine Wij for a network with multiple miners according
to different  tie-breaking rules. It  is assumed that all forks
cause chain ties.
First-Seen Rule: We assume that miner i starts a round,
and then miner j causes a chain tie in the same round. Let
pi,j,k be the probability that miner k mines on the block
generated by miner i. The time Tij  it  takes for the block
generated by miner i to reach miner j is assumed to be a
fixed value that depends only on i and j.
When Tik < Tjk,  regardless of  the time when miner
j generates the block, the block generated by miner i will
reach miner k first, and hence, pi,j,k = 1. Similarly, when
Tik < Tij +Tjk, the block generated by miner j will reach
miner k first, and hence, pi,j,k = 0.  In other cases,  the
following equation holds:

4.3.2. Simulation Settings. The simulator used in this vali-
dation was an extended version of a network simulator com-
posed of two miners. The number of miners was set to 10.
The hashrate distribution was based on that of Bitcoin [20].
The hashrate distribution settings are shown in Fig. 5.
In this validation, the ratios of the average block prop-
agation time to the average block generation interval, d/T ,
were varied among the values of 0.01, 0.04, 0.07, and 0.1.
These settings cover most blockchain systems; for instance,
in  the case of Bitcoin,  d/T  is approximately equal  to
0.00576, whereas in the case of Ethereum, d/T = 0.068 [5],
[21]. The block propagation time distribution among the
different miners followed an exponential distribution [2],

whereas the block propagation time to oneself was set  to
0. Additionally, the previously described tie-breaking rules,
i.e., the first-seen rule, random rule, and last-generated rule,
were examined.
Each simulation consisted of 10 billion rounds.
4.3.3. Validation Results. We conducted 50 simulation ex-
periments for each validation target. The errors between the
simulation and model-based calculation results are shown in
Tables 3 and 4. Here, error is defined as the relative error as
in (53). The difference from the previous validation is that
the number of elements in the LF vector is changed from
two to ten, and we investigate not only the LF vector but
also the round-start-rate vector.
First, we examine the round start rate. It can be seen that
the model-based calculations match the simulation results
with high accuracy under all conditions.
Next, we examine mining fairness. From the tables,  it
can be seen that the model-based calculation method is able
to compute mining fairness with high accuracy. It can also
be observed that the accuracy deteriorates as d/T increases.
Furthermore, it is found that the accuracy of the mining
fairness calculations is not as high as that of  the round-
start-rate calculations or of the mining fairness calculations
for a network composed of two miners. This is because the
mining fairness calculation requires Wij .
Additionally, it is observed that the first-seen rule is more
accurate than the random rule or the last-generated rule. This
finding indicates that  the calculation of Wij based on the
first-seen rule is superior.

This is because the influence of
blocks up to the second one in a given round is stronger
under the first-seen rule.
We also compared our proposed model-based calculation
with a state-of-the-art method. This comparison method is
the same as that proposed except that the latter considers the
round start rate [13]. By contrast,  the comparison method
automatically assumes that the round start rate is equal to
the hashrate. Tables 5 and 6 present the results. As can be
seen, our model-based calculation significantly improves the.

In this section, we present a detailed analysis of mining
fairness using the model-based calculation. First, we analyze
mining fairness for a simple network composed of two min-
ers (Section 5.1). Next, we examine the impact of hashrate
distribution on mining fairness (Section 5.2). Finally, we
reassess existing blockchain network improvement methods
from the perspective of mining fairness (Section 5.3).

5.1. Network Composed of Two Miners
In this section, we provide a thorough examination of
mining fairness in a simple network that includes only two
miners. Analyzing such a network offers two advantages:
there is no need to consider tie-breaking rules, and it allows
for a more general analysis without  substituting specific
values for the parameters. We investigate the properties of
mining fairness in this network using the proposed model-
based calculation.
5.1.1. Analysis Subject. We analyze a network composed
of two miners (MinerA and MinerB). The block propa-

5.1.2. Analysis Results. We conducted an analysis based
on the model-based calculation using Wij (see Section 4.2),
which was validated in a network composed of two miners.
First, we examine the local mining fairness LF1(A)
for MinerA. Table 7 and Fig. 6 illustrate LF1(A) with
variations in d/T ,  i.e.,  the ratio of the block propagation
time to the average block generation interval, and in αA,
i.e.,  the proportion of hashrate of MinerA.  It  is evident
herein that as d/T increases, mining fairness deteriorates.
Next, we focus on αA. Considering symmetry, we per-
form analyses with αA varying from 0.5 to 1, by which
LF1  is observed to concave upward. This  is because as
αA increases, MinerA gains power to take rewards from
MinerB, but the number of blocks generated by MinerB
decreases accordingly.
Then, we investigate the maximum value of LF1 and the
corresponding αA for various values of d/T using a ternary
search. Table 8 shows the results. It can be observed that
as d/T increases,  the maximum value of LF1  increases,
whereas αA at which  this maximum value  is achieved
decreases.
Next, we examine the local mining fairness of MinerA,
i.e., LF2(A). Table 9 and Fig. 7 show LF2(A) with respect
to variations  in d/T and αA.  It  is evident  that as d/T
increases, mining fairness deteriorates.
Afterward, we focus on αA. Unlike LF1, LF2 does
not exhibit any symmetry. When αA is varied from 0.0 to
0.5, LF2 increases monotonically. When αA is varied from
0.5 to 1, LF2 concaves upwards. Therefore, we investigate
the maximum value of LF2 and the corresponding αA for
various values of d/T using a ternary search. Table 10 shows
the results.  It can be observed that as d/T increases,  the
maximum value of LF2 increases, whereas αA at which this
maximum value is achieved decreases. It  is also observed
that αA at which the maximum value of LF2 is achieved is
slightly smaller than that for LF1.
Finally, we examine global mining fairness. For a net-
work composed of two miners,  the property GF1 can be

0.5 and 1. It is observed that GF2 increases monotonically
as αA increases.
5.2.  Impact of Hashrate Distribution
In this section, we explore how the hashrate distribution
affects mining fairness. Different hashrate distributions can
result in varying degrees of mining fairness. For example, it
is expected that the revenues of miners with larger hashrates
will tend to be higher. This is because the blocks of these
miners are more likely to be included in the main chain in
the event of ties. We aim to analyze such patterns thoroughly
by examining various hashrate distribution scenarios and
their impacts on revenue distribution.
5.2.1. Analysis Subjects.
Block Propagation Time and Average Block Genera-
tion Interval: We perform our analysis for various ratios
of  the propagation time to the average block generation

Hashrate Distribution and Number of Miners The
number of miners is set to 100. We analyze six distributions
of the hashrate ratio as shown in Fig. 9 [20]. Specifically,
we first allocate the hashrate ratios of the Pools, excluding
Others,  to the miners. Then,  the remaining hashrate ratio
is evenly distributed among the remaining miners, ensuring
that  the  total hashrate  ratio of  these miners equals  the
hashrate ratio of Others. The value for Others corresponds
to the total hashrate ratio of solo miners.
5.2.2. Analysis Results. We conducted an analysis based
on the model-based calculation using Wij  for a network
composed of ten miners (see Section 4.3).
First, we confirmed that LF2 becomes a linear function
of α. Fig. 10 shows scatter plots of LF2 with the hashrate
distribution of Bitcoin under the first-seen rule. It is evident
that (α, LF2) lies on a straight line regardless of the con-
ditions. This is also true for any analysis subject, based on
the correlation coefficient, which averages 0.999995, with
a standard deviation of 0.00000667953. Then, using the
least-squares method, we determined the slope of each line.

5.3. Reassessing Existing Blockchain Network Im-
provement Methods
Despite the significance of analyzing mining fairness,
existing methods for analyzing and improving blockchain
networks have not been evaluated from the perspective of

TABLE 15: GF1 and
first-seen rule  rand
GF1  GF2  GF1
Bitcoin  0.000833972  0.00539058  0.00083256
Dogecoin  0.000694512  0.00489436  0.00069322

mining fairness. Other metrics such as fork rate, stale rate, X
percentile block propagation time, communication overhead,
and average propagation time cannot be used as substitutes
for mining fairness. For instance, even if the overall stale
rate is high, mining fairness can be achieved if all the miners
have equal stale rates.
In this section, we reevaluate existing methods for im-
proving blockchain networks from the perspective of mining
fairness. We also examine the relationship between block
propagation time and mining fairness. Furthermore, we an-
alyze the partial application of these methods to improve
blockchain networks.
5.3.1. Analysis Subjects. The analysis subjects are as fol-
lows:
Normal Block Relay 2019 (Normal 2019)
A block relay protocol  that was widely used
in Bitcoin. Herein, a node that has received a
block sends an inv message to neighboring
nodes to inform them of the new block. Nodes
that  are unaware of  the block request  for  it
by sending getdata messages. Upon receiv-
ing the getdata message, the node sends the
block. The network parameter settings (network
latency and bandwidth) are the same as those in
2019 [22].
Normal Block Relay 2015 (Normal 2015)
Almost  the same as Normal 2019 except  for
the network configuration. Network parameter
settings (network latency and bandwidth) are
the same as those in 2015 [22]. The purpose
of including this network improvement method
in the analysis is to investigate the effects of
network development on mining fairness.
Compact Block Relay 2019 (Compact 2019)
A block relay protocol  that  is intended to re-
duce traffic [23].  Its concept  is very simple:
The nodes send compact blocks instead of full
blocks. Compact blocks contain short  transac-
tion IDs,  not  raw transactions. When nodes
receive compact  blocks,  they attempt  to re-
construct  the full blocks.  If the reconstruction
fails,  the nodes require the transactions corre-
sponding to the short transaction IDs. Compact
Block Relay has  two modes: high-bandwidth
mode and low-bandwidth mode. Nodes using
the high-bandwidth mode send compact blocks

without  sending inv messages and verifying

bandwidth mode. The network parameter set-
tings (network latency and bandwidth) are the
same as those in 2019.
Graphene 2019
A block relay protocol that enables lower traf-
fic consumption than that  for Compact Block
Relay [24]. Graphene uses Bloom filters [25]
and invertible Bloom lookup tables (IBLTs) [26]
instead of full blocks. The success rate of decod-
ing IBLTs is very high when proper parameters
are set, and the sizes of IBLTs are lower than
those of compact blocks. The specific proto-
col and implementation of Graphene are very
complex. Fortunately, a study by Muhammad
et al. provides an entire protocol that is easy to
understand [27].

The network parameter settings
(network latency and bandwidth) are the same
as those in 2019.
Relay Network 2019 (Relay 2019)
A network for fast block propagation. Nodes
can inform blocks  throughout  the blockchain
network by using a relay network. There are
several  types of  relay networks. The type of
relay networks we consider is the same as those
investigated by Otsuki et al [28]. This type of
relay network conforms to the basic structure
of relay networks. When nodes generate blocks,
they utilize a relay server to send those blocks.
The relay servers  then share the blocks with
other  relay servers and clients. The network
parameter settings (network latency and band-
width) are the same as those in 2019.
The calculation of mining fairness also accounts for tie-
breaking rules. As in the previous analysis, we perform our
analyses under  the first-seen rule,  random rule, and last-
generated rule.
5.3.2. Analysis Method and Setup.  The analysis was
conducted by combining blockchain network simulations
and the model-based calculation. First, we simulated the
blockchain network improvement methods. Subsequently,
from these  simulations, we obtained  the proportion of
hashrate for each miner and the block propagation time.
Using the simulation results, we performed the model-based
calculation to determine mining fairness (Fig. 13).

model
r  mining 
methods  simulator  based
fairness
calculation
Figure 13: Combining blockchain network simulations and
model-based calculation.
We used SimBlock, a blockchain network simulator [19],
to determine the block propagation time for each method.
In SimBlock, miners had attributes such as region, hashrate,
and neighbor nodes. There were a  total of  six regions.
The bandwidth and latency were determined between the
regions. The hashrate distribution followed a normal distri-
8
bution with a mean of 4 · 10  (/s) and a standard deviation
8
of 1 · 10  (/s). Each miner had eight outbound connections
and up to 125 inbound connections. The number of miners
was set to 1000, and the average block generation interval,
T , was set to 600 s.
For Normal 2019, Normal 2015, Compact 2019, and
Graphene 2019,  the  implementations were  the same as
those provided by Muhammad et al [27]. For Relay 2019,
the implementation was referenced from that of Otsuki et
al [28]. Specifically, relay servers were newly installed in
each region. The bandwidth for the communication between
the relay servers was reduced to 1/10. Additionally, the relay
servers propagated blocks in parallel with the relay clients
within the same region. The relay clients sent blocks to the
relay servers only when the clients generated them.
We also set  the parameters for each block size (full
blocks, compact blocks, and graphene blocks) and the suc-
cess rate of reconstructing full blocks in the case of Compact
Block Relay and Graphene using log data provided by
Muhammad et al [27], [29].
For  the block propagation time between miners, each
miner generated and propagated blocks 10 times, and the
average propagation time was calculated.
5.3.3. Analysis Results. We analyze the aforementioned
methods  from the perspective of mining fairness. First,
we investigate global mining fairness and the relationships
between mining fairness, block propagation time, and fork
rate. Afterward, we analyze the effect of partial application
on mining fairness.
Before analyzing global mining fairness, we first define
the hashrate-weighted average block propagation time, TW ,
which we will use in the subsequent analyses:

6.1. Related Work
Mining Fairness for Unintentional Forks: Croman et
al. proposed several metrics and solutions to address the
scalability problems of blockchains [9]. They highlighted
mining fairness as a potentially more valuable metric, de-
spite its difficulty in measurement.
Since then, numerous studies have attempted to analyze
mining fairness [10]–[14], but none have accurately captured
the impact of forks on mining fairness. Consequently,  the
analyses conducted thus far diverged from the actual values
observed in blockchain networks. Herein, we discuss each
of these studies in detail.
Kanda et al. introduced the concept of effective hashrate,
based on the idea that miners cannot contribute to the main
chain until they receive the latest block [10]. They calculated
the effective hashrate by multiplying the original hashrate by
the ratio of the time required to receive a block, to the block
generation interval. They asserted that the proportion of the
effective hashrate is equal  to the block reward rate. The
problem with their calculation method is that it ignores the
capability of miners to still contribute to the main chain even
if they have not received the latest block. Blocks generated
by miners who have not received the latest block can cause
chain ties and may still be included in the main chain.
Moreover, miners can increase their block reward rate by
invalidating the opponent’s block during chain ties. The
same problem arose in the study by Tong et al [15].
Jiang et al. analyzed mining fairness based on a concept
similar to that proposed by Kanda et al [11]. They calculated
the average block reception time for each miner and defined
the maximum difference  in the average block reception
times as mining fairness. However, their concept of mining
fairness evidently deviates from reality.
Xiao et al. proposed a model-based approach to an-
alyzing mining fairness  in a blockchain network for  the
analysis of the blockchain network connectivity [13]. The

In this paper, we propose an efficient method for calcu-
lating mining fairness, one of the key metrics in blockchain,
by approximating a complex blockchain network with a
simpler network, where the number of blocks per round is
at most  two. We also analyzed mining fairness based on
several aspects. Specifically, we examined mining fairness
in a simple network composed of two miners and the impact
of the hashrate distribution on mining fairness. Finally, we
analyzed existing blockchain network improvement methods
from the perspective of mining fairness.
